clinicalStem: A 72-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by his family with a 3-week history of progressive confusion, gait instability, and urinary incontinence. He had a ventriculoperitoneal shunt placed 8 years ago for normal-pressure hydrocephalus, which had been working well. On examination he is disorientated to time and place, has a broad-based shuffling gait, and cannot suppress urge to void. His MRI brain shows marked ventricular enlargement larger than his previous imaging. What is the most important next step in management?
options: Start donepezil for presumed Alzheimer's disease
Refer to a memory clinic for formal cognitive assessment in 6 weeks
Urgent neurosurgical review for shunt malfunction
Prescribe oxybutynin for urge incontinence
Commence a trial of levodopa to distinguish NPH from Parkinson's disease
explanation: This patient presents with the classic triad of NPH (wet, wobbly, wacky) and has a previously placed VP shunt with new ventricular enlargement on MRI, strongly suggesting acute shunt malfunction. Urgent neurosurgical review is required because obstructed CSF flow can cause rapid neurological deterioration and is potentially fatal if not addressed. Treating symptomatically without addressing the mechanical cause would be dangerous and inappropriate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Donepezil addresses Alzheimer's disease, not the reversible shunt malfunction causing this clinical picture; starting it would delay life-saving intervention.
Outpatient memory clinic referral is wholly inappropriate given evidence of acute shunt failure with progressive neurological decline requiring urgent surgical assessment.
This is the correct answer — urgent neurosurgical review for shunt malfunction is the priority given the enlarged ventricles and known shunt history.
Oxybutynin treats the symptom of incontinence but completely ignores the underlying surgical emergency causing all three symptoms.
A levodopa trial is used to help distinguish NPH from Parkinson's in uncertain cases; this patient has an established diagnosis and a clear mechanical cause for deterioration.
clinicalPearl: In a patient with a known VP shunt who develops worsening NPH triad plus ventricular enlargement on imaging, always suspect shunt malfunction and seek urgent neurosurgical review before any other intervention.
keyClue: Known VP shunt + new ventricular enlargement larger than baseline on MRI
commonExamTrap: Attributing the cognitive decline to Alzheimer's disease and starting a cholinesterase inhibitor without checking for treatable structural causes.
missedConcept: Shunt malfunction is a surgical emergency and must always be excluded before attributing deterioration to progression of the underlying dementia.
redFlagReason: Acute VP shunt malfunction causes rapidly rising intracranial pressure and can be fatal if not urgently corrected by neurosurgery.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old retired teacher presents with an 18-month history of progressive forgetfulness, difficulty finding words, and getting lost in familiar areas. Her MMSE score is 21/30. MRI brain shows hippocampal atrophy bilaterally. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis reveals reduced amyloid-β42 and elevated tau. Her general practitioner asks which pharmacological agent is most appropriate to initiate at this stage.
options: Memantine 10 mg twice daily
Donepezil 5 mg once daily at night
Haloperidol 0.5 mg twice daily
Rivastigmine patch 13.3 mg/24 hours
Lorazepam 1 mg at night for anxiety related to memory loss
explanation: This patient has mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease (MMSE 21/30) with biomarker confirmation (low CSF amyloid-β42, elevated tau). Donepezil, a cholinesterase inhibitor, is the first-line agent for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease. It is started at 5 mg once nightly and can be titrated to 10 mg after 4 weeks if tolerated. Memantine is reserved for moderate-to-severe disease or as an add-on.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Memantine is indicated for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's (MMSE < 15) or as combination therapy; it is not first-line for mild-to-moderate disease.
This is the correct answer — donepezil 5 mg at night is the appropriate first-line cholinesterase inhibitor for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease.
Haloperidol is an antipsychotic with significant side effects in elderly patients; it has no role in the initial management of cognitive symptoms in Alzheimer's disease.
The high-dose rivastigmine patch (13.3 mg/24 h) is reserved for patients already on and tolerating the 9.5 mg/24 h patch; it is not an appropriate starting agent.
Benzodiazepines such as lorazepam cause significant cognitive impairment and are contraindicated in dementia; they worsen the underlying condition.
clinicalPearl: Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) are first-line for mild-to-moderate Alzheimer's disease; memantine is added or substituted in moderate-to-severe disease.
keyClue: MMSE 21/30 (mild-to-moderate range) with CSF biomarkers confirming Alzheimer's pathology
commonExamTrap: Choosing memantine as first-line — it is only first-line for severe disease or as an adjunct to a cholinesterase inhibitor in moderate disease.
missedConcept: The stage of Alzheimer's disease determines which pharmacological agent is appropriate; initiating the wrong class at the wrong stage is a common exam error.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 5-day history of ascending weakness in both legs that has now reached her trunk. Three weeks ago she had a diarrhoeal illness. On examination she has bilateral lower limb areflexia, mild facial weakness, and bilateral ptosis. Vital capacity measured at the bedside is 1.2 L (predicted 3.6 L). She states she feels she cannot take a deep breath. Nerve conduction studies show prolonged distal latencies and reduced conduction velocities. What is the single most important next management step?
options: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily
Arrange urgent MRI of the whole spine to exclude cord compression
Admit to ICU for elective intubation and mechanical ventilation
Administer intravenous immunoglobulin and monitor on a general ward
Perform lumbar puncture and await CSF results before any further intervention
explanation: This patient has Guillain-Barré syndrome with rapidly ascending paralysis, bulbar involvement (facial weakness, ptosis), and critically reduced vital capacity at 33% of predicted (rule of 20-30-40: VC < 20 mL/kg, MIF worse than −30 cmH2O, MEP < 40 cmH2O triggers intubation consideration). A VC of 1.2 L in a woman of this size is a clear indication for elective intubation before respiratory failure occurs. Waiting until she decompensates risks a crash intubation with significantly higher risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Corticosteroids are not beneficial in Guillain-Barré syndrome and may worsen outcome; they are explicitly contraindicated for GBS.

MRI spine is relevant if cord compression is suspected, but this presentation — ascending paralysis with areflexia, post-infective course, and NCS findings — is classic GBS; delaying airway management for imaging would be dangerous.
This is the correct answer — elective ICU admission and intubation before respiratory failure is the priority given critically reduced vital capacity with bulbar involvement.
IVIG is the correct disease-modifying treatment but administering it on a general ward with a VC of 1.2 L and bulbar signs risks unwitnessed respiratory arrest; ICU monitoring comes first.
CSF in GBS classically shows albuminocytological dissociation, but this result will not change the immediate airway priority; deferring intubation to await CSF results risks respiratory arrest.
clinicalPearl: In GBS, the 20-30-40 rule guides intubation: vital capacity < 20 mL/kg, maximum inspiratory force worse than −30 cmH2O, or maximum expiratory pressure < 40 cmH2O — electively intubate before crisis.
keyClue: Vital capacity 1.2 L (33% predicted) with ascending paralysis, bulbar signs, and areflexia in GBS
commonExamTrap: Starting IVIG immediately and monitoring on a general ward, ignoring the impending respiratory failure indicated by critically low vital capacity.
missedConcept: Airway and breathing take precedence over disease-modifying therapy; GBS patients can deteriorate rapidly and elective intubation is far safer than emergency intubation.
redFlagReason: GBS with VC < 1.5 L or < 20 mL/kg and bulbar signs represents imminent respiratory failure requiring elective intubation in an ICU setting.

clinicalStem: A 45-year-old woman with known myasthenia gravis (on pyridostigmine 60 mg four times daily) is brought in by ambulance with acute breathlessness and increasing difficulty swallowing. She was started on ciprofloxacin 3 days ago for a urinary tract infection. On examination she is using accessory muscles, SpO2 is 88% on air, she cannot hold her head up, and her voice is nasal. Bedside spirometry shows FVC 0.9 L (predicted 3.4 L). What is the most urgent management step?
options: Increase pyridostigmine dose to 90 mg four times daily
Administer atropine 600 mcg IV to manage secretions
Secure the airway and arrange ICU admission for ventilatory support
Give IV methylprednisolone 1 g and observe on a respiratory ward
Perform a Tensilon (edrophonium) test to confirm myasthenic crisis
explanation: This patient is in myasthenic crisis — a life-threatening exacerbation characterised by respiratory failure (FVC < 1.0 L, SpO2 88%, accessory muscle use) precipitated by ciprofloxacin (a known precipitant of MG exacerbation). Immediate airway protection and ventilatory support in ICU is the priority. Subsequent treatments (plasma exchange or IVIG) address the underlying crisis once the airway is secured.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing pyridostigmine risks precipitating cholinergic crisis (excessive secretions, bradycardia, fasciculations) and will not quickly reverse the neuromuscular blockade causing the respiratory failure.
Atropine may reduce secretions but does not treat the cause; this is myasthenic not cholinergic crisis, and atropine alone cannot prevent respiratory arrest.
This is the correct answer — immediate airway management and ICU admission are the life-saving priorities in myasthenic crisis with SpO2 88% and FVC 0.9 L.
High-dose steroids can paradoxically worsen myasthenic weakness acutely; they are not the first step in crisis management and do not address the immediate respiratory failure.
The Tensilon test carries the risk of bradyarrhythmia and is unnecessary when the clinical diagnosis is clear; performing it delays life-saving ventilatory support.
clinicalPearl: Myasthenic crisis is a medical emergency; FVC < 1.0 L or rapidly falling is an absolute indication for elective intubation. Fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, and macrolides are known precipitants of MG exacerbation and should be avoided.
keyClue: Known MG + FVC 0.9 L + SpO2 88% after starting ciprofloxacin (a neuromuscular junction-impairing antibiotic)
commonExamTrap: Increasing pyridostigmine, which could precipitate a cholinergic crisis on top of the myasthenic crisis.
missedConcept: Airway and breathing must be secured before any immunomodulatory treatment in myasthenic crisis, and the precipitant (offending drug) must be identified and stopped.
redFlagReason: Myasthenic crisis with SpO2 88% and FVC < 1.0 L represents imminent respiratory arrest requiring immediate intubation.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man with a 12-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus presents with a 6-month history of bilateral burning pain and tingling in his feet, worse at night. He describes the sensation as 'walking on hot coals'. Neurological examination reveals reduced pin-prick and vibration sense in a stocking distribution with absent ankle jerks bilaterally. Nerve conduction studies confirm a sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy. HbA1c is 89 mmol/mol. His only regular medication is metformin. Which pharmacological agent is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his neuropathic pain?
options: Tramadol 50 mg as required
Amitriptyline 10 mg at night, titrated to response
Gabapentin 300 mg three times daily from day one
Morphine sulphate 10 mg twice daily (modified release)
Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily with food
explanation: Amitriptyline (or duloxetine) is recommended as first-line pharmacotherapy for painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. It is started at a low dose (10 mg at night) and titrated slowly to minimise side effects. The low starting dose also takes advantage of its sedative properties (helping with the night-time pain component) while reducing anticholinergic adverse effects.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Tramadol is a weak opioid and is not first-line for neuropathic pain; it carries risks of dependence, seizure threshold lowering, and serotonin syndrome, particularly in this context.
This is the correct answer — amitriptyline is a well-established first-line agent for painful diabetic neuropathy, started at low dose and titrated.

Gabapentin 300 mg three times daily is too high a starting dose for most patients and risks sedation, falls, and dizziness; it should be titrated up from 100 mg three times daily and is a second-line option in many guidelines after tricyclics or duloxetine.
Strong opioids are not first- or second-line for painful neuropathy; they carry addiction potential and their benefit in neuropathic pain is modest.
NSAIDs are ineffective for neuropathic pain and are relatively contraindicated in diabetic patients due to risks of renal impairment and fluid retention.
clinicalPearl: First-line agents for painful diabetic neuropathy are amitriptyline or duloxetine; pregabalin and gabapentin are alternatives. NSAIDs and simple analgesics are ineffective for neuropathic pain.
keyClue: Bilateral burning pain in stocking distribution at night with reduced pin-prick/vibration and absent ankle jerks in a long-standing diabetic
commonExamTrap: Prescribing NSAIDs or tramadol for neuropathic pain, which is a different pain mechanism from nociceptive pain.
missedConcept: Neuropathic pain requires agents acting on central pain modulation (tricyclics, SNRIs, anticonvulsants) rather than conventional analgesics.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old male smoker with a 30-pack-year history presents with a 3-month history of proximal lower limb weakness and difficulty rising from a chair. He also reports a dry mouth and erectile dysfunction. On examination, proximal muscle power is 3/5 in both lower limbs. Importantly, repeated muscle testing shows temporary improvement in strength with repeated contractions. Deep tendon reflexes are absent but transiently appear after repeated exercise. Nerve conduction studies show a low compound muscle action potential (CMAP) that markedly increments (>100%) with high-frequency stimulation. What is the single most important next investigation?
options: Anti-acetylcholine receptor antibodies
Creatine kinase (CK) level
CT chest with contrast
MRI brain with gadolinium
Edrophonium (Tensilon) test
explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is a paraneoplastic condition in over 50% of cases, most commonly associated with small cell lung cancer (SCLC). In a 62-year-old smoker with this classic LEMS picture (proximal weakness, autonomic features, facilitation on repetitive stimulation), urgent CT chest is mandatory to exclude underlying malignancy. Anti-VGCC antibodies confirm the diagnosis but treatment of the underlying cancer is the most important management step.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Anti-AChR antibodies are positive in myasthenia gravis, not LEMS; the clinical and neurophysiological picture here (facilitation rather than decrement, autonomic features) is characteristic of LEMS.
CK may be mildly elevated in some myopathies but is not the priority investigation here; the neurophysiology points to a NMJ disorder, not a primary muscle disease.
This is the correct answer — CT chest is urgent to identify small cell lung cancer, the most common underlying malignancy in paraneoplastic LEMS.
MRI brain would be relevant if central nervous system metastases or an intrinsic brain lesion were suspected; proximal limb weakness with NMJ neurophysiology does not point to a central lesion.
Edrophonium test is used for myasthenia gravis; in LEMS the response is variable and the test carries cardiac risks. Neurophysiology has already established the diagnosis.
clinicalPearl: LEMS in a middle-aged smoker is a paraneoplastic syndrome until proven otherwise — always perform urgent CT chest to exclude small cell lung carcinoma.
keyClue: Proximal weakness with facilitation (strength improves with repetition) and autonomic features (dry mouth) plus high-frequency CMAP increment >100% in a heavy smoker
commonExamTrap: Confusing LEMS with myasthenia gravis — MG shows fatigability (decrement on repetitive stimulation) whereas LEMS shows facilitation (increment).
missedConcept: LEMS is commonly paraneoplastic; identifying and treating the underlying tumour is the most important step in management and may improve neurological symptoms.

subtopic: Identifying and managing delirium in a known dementia patient
clinicalStem: An 80-year-old woman with known mild Alzheimer's dementia is brought to A&E by her carer because she has become acutely aggressive and confused over the past 12 hours. Her baseline is orientated to person but not always to time. Today she is shouting, pulling at her IV cannula, and seeing insects on the wall. Temperature is 38.6°C, heart rate 106 bpm, BP 134/88 mmHg. Urinalysis shows leukocytes ++ and nitrites positive. Her usual medications include donepezil and amlodipine. What is the single most important management priority?
options: Increase donepezil to 10 mg to improve cognition acutely
Prescribe lorazepam 2 mg IV for agitation
Identify and treat the underlying precipitant (urinary tract infection)
Arrange urgent CT brain to rule out new cerebrovascular event
Start haloperidol 5 mg IM for the acute psychotic episode
explanation: This patient is experiencing delirium superimposed on pre-existing dementia, precipitated by a urinary tract infection (fever, tachycardia, positive urinalysis). The cornerstone of delirium management is identifying and treating the underlying cause. Antibiotics for the UTI, adequate hydration, and supportive measures should be the immediate priority. Sedative and antipsychotic medications should only be used as a last resort for safety.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Increasing donepezil will not acutely reverse delirium and may worsen cholinergic side effects; this is not an appropriate acute intervention.

Lorazepam at this dose in an elderly patient with dementia risks respiratory depression, paradoxical disinhibition, and worsened confusion; benzodiazepines are not first-line for delirium in the elderly.
This is the correct answer — treating the underlying precipitant (UTI with antibiotics and fluids) is the most important and effective step in managing delirium.
CT brain may be warranted if no other cause is found or if focal neurology is present, but it is not the priority when a clear infectious precipitant is already identified.

High-dose haloperidol IM in a patient with dementia (and possible Lewy body dementia) carries the risk of severe extrapyramidal reactions and increased mortality; even if needed, much lower doses and only after other measures have failed.
clinicalPearl: Delirium is always secondary to an underlying cause — find it and treat it. In elderly patients with dementia, a UTI is a common precipitant. Sedatives treat the symptom, not the cause, and carry significant risks in this population.
keyClue: Acute onset (12 hours) of confusion with fever, tachycardia, and positive urinalysis in a patient with known dementia
commonExamTrap: Attributing the acute behavioural change to worsening dementia rather than recognising superimposed delirium from an acute medical cause.
missedConcept: Delirium has a fluctuating acute course; dementia is chronic and progressive. Any acute change in a dementia patient mandates a search for reversible precipitants.
redFlagReason: Untreated sepsis (UTI with systemic features) in an elderly patient with dementia can rapidly progress to severe sepsis; prompt antibiotic treatment is urgent.

clinicalStem: A 76-year-old woman with moderate Alzheimer's disease (MMSE 14/30) has been prescribed donepezil for the past year. Her family reports increasing agitation, verbal aggression, and nocturnal wandering over the past 2 months despite a stable home environment. Non-pharmacological strategies including structured daily activity, environmental modification, and redirection have been tried for 6 weeks without significant benefit. She has no history of cardiac arrhythmia. What is the most appropriate pharmacological next step for her behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD)?
options: Add memantine 10 mg twice daily
Prescribe diazepam 5 mg at night for agitation and sleep
Stop donepezil as it may be causing the agitation
Prescribe low-dose risperidone with regular review and informed consent discussion
Start sertraline 50 mg daily for assumed underlying depression
explanation: After failure of non-pharmacological measures, low-dose risperidone (an atypical antipsychotic) is the most evidence-based pharmacological option for agitation and aggression in Alzheimer's disease BPSD. It should be used at the lowest effective dose, with regular review, clear documentation that benefits outweigh risks (including small increased risk of stroke and death in dementia patients), and informed consent. Memantine is a cognitive agent, not primarily used for BPSD.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Memantine has some benefit for overall function in moderate-to-severe Alzheimer's disease but is not the primary treatment for agitation and BPSD; it does not address the acute behavioural symptoms.
Benzodiazepines worsen cognition, increase fall risk, and can paradoxically disinhibit; they are contraindicated for chronic BPSD management.
Donepezil should not be stopped arbitrarily; there is no strong evidence that cholinesterase inhibitors cause agitation, and stopping may precipitate further cognitive decline.
This is the correct answer — low-dose risperidone is the recommended pharmacological option for BPSD when non-pharmacological measures have failed, used with appropriate risk counselling and review.

Sertraline may help if there is a clear depressive component to the BPSD, but agitation and nocturnal wandering without prominent low mood are not primarily depressive features; risperidone is more appropriate here.
clinicalPearl: For BPSD, always try non-pharmacological measures first. When medications are required, low-dose risperidone is the most evidence-based option for agitation in Alzheimer's disease, but patients must be counselled about the small increased risk of stroke and death.
keyClue: Moderate Alzheimer's disease with persistent agitation and aggression despite 6 weeks of non-pharmacological interventions
commonExamTrap: Using benzodiazepines for BPSD, which worsen cognition and increase falls in elderly patients.
missedConcept: Antipsychotics carry a class-wide warning in dementia (increased risk of stroke and death) and must only be used after failed non-pharmacological measures, at lowest effective dose, with regular review.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 38-year-old pregnant woman (28 weeks gestation) presents with a 6-week history of bilateral hand numbness and tingling, predominantly in the thumb, index, and middle fingers. Symptoms are worse at night and she frequently wakes to shake her hands. Tinel's sign is positive bilaterally. There is no wasting of the thenar eminence and power is normal. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Urgent referral for surgical carpal tunnel decompression
Nerve conduction studies followed by surgical decompression
Night splints (neutral wrist position) and watchful waiting
Local corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel
Oral prednisolone 20 mg daily for 2 weeks
explanation: This is classical carpal tunnel syndrome in pregnancy, a common and usually reversible condition due to fluid retention and oedema compressing the median nerve. In the absence of thenar wasting or severe motor deficit, and given the obstetric context where surgery is to be avoided, conservative management with neutral-position wrist splints worn at night is the appropriate first step. Symptoms typically resolve postpartum.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Surgical decompression is reserved for severe or refractory cases with thenar wasting or failed conservative treatment; it is not appropriate as the initial step in a pregnant patient without motor loss.
Nerve conduction studies can confirm the diagnosis but are not necessary before initiating conservative treatment in a clinically classic case; surgery is inappropriate at this stage.
This is the correct answer — night splints in neutral wrist position are safe, effective, and the appropriate first-line treatment for CTS in pregnancy without motor deficit.
Corticosteroid injection is a valid option in non-pregnant patients who fail splinting; in pregnancy it is generally avoided unless conservative measures fail and delivery is not imminent.
Systemic oral steroids are inappropriate for CTS; they expose the fetus to unnecessary risk and are not indicated for this condition.
clinicalPearl: CTS in pregnancy is usually caused by fluid retention and often resolves postpartum. Neutral-position wrist splints worn at night are safe and effective first-line treatment; surgical decompression is reserved for severe cases with motor involvement.
keyClue: Pregnancy + classic median nerve distribution + positive Tinel's + no thenar wasting or motor deficit
commonExamTrap: Immediately referring for surgical decompression when conservative management has not been tried and there is no motor deficit.
missedConcept: In pregnancy-related CTS without motor involvement, conservative management with wrist splints is almost always the right answer; surgery should be the last resort.

clinicalStem: A 14-year-old boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) is reviewed in clinic. He is wheelchair dependent and on deflazacort (a corticosteroid). His parents are concerned because over the past year he has developed progressive shortness of breath, especially when lying flat, and frequent morning headaches. Spirometry shows FVC 45% predicted. Overnight pulse oximetry reveals periods of oxygen desaturation to 82%. What complication has developed, and what is the most appropriate management?
options: Aspiration pneumonia; start prophylactic antibiotics and speech therapy
Cardiac failure secondary to DMD cardiomyopathy; commence ACE inhibitor
Nocturnal hypoventilation; initiate non-invasive ventilation (NIV/BiPAP)
OSA due to obesity from steroids; refer for CPAP titration
Pulmonary embolism; arrange urgent CTPA and anticoagulation
explanation: As DMD progresses, respiratory muscle weakness causes nocturnal hypoventilation characterised by orthopnoea, morning headaches (from CO2 retention during sleep), and nocturnal desaturations on oximetry. FVC 45% predicted confirms significant restrictive ventilatory impairment. The appropriate management is initiation of non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) which reduces nocturnal CO2 retention, improves sleep quality, and significantly prolongs survival. CPAP provides positive pressure but does not augment ventilation and is insufficient.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Aspiration pneumonia is a risk in DMD with bulbar weakness but would typically present with fever, productive cough, and infiltrates on imaging; the symptom pattern here points to nocturnal hypoventilation.

DMD cardiomyopathy is important to monitor and treat, but cardiac failure alone does not explain the morning headaches and nocturnal desaturations; respiratory muscle weakness is the primary issue here.
This is the correct answer — nocturnal hypoventilation from respiratory muscle weakness in DMD is treated with non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP), which augments breathing and relieves CO2 retention.
CPAP provides continuous airway pressure and may help obstructive apneas but does not support respiratory muscles; BiPAP is required when hypoventilation (not obstruction) is the mechanism.

PE would cause acute breathlessness and hypoxia but not the chronic pattern of morning headaches and progressive orthopnoea over a year in a patient with known severe restrictive ventilatory impairment.
clinicalPearl: Morning headaches + orthopnoea + nocturnal desaturations in DMD = nocturnal hypoventilation from respiratory muscle weakness. Treat with BiPAP (non-invasive ventilation), not CPAP, as ventilatory support is required.
keyClue: Morning headaches (CO2 retention), orthopnoea, nocturnal desaturations, and FVC 45% in a wheelchair-bound DMD patient
commonExamTrap: Choosing CPAP instead of BiPAP — CPAP does not assist ventilation, which is what is needed when respiratory muscles are failing.
missedConcept: Nocturnal hypoventilation is a major and life-limiting complication of DMD; regular spirometry and overnight oximetry should prompt early BiPAP initiation to extend survival and quality of life.

sourceLecture: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System
sourceFile: 4.1 Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System.pdf
clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman wakes up with sudden onset of complete right-sided facial weakness involving both the upper and lower face. She is unable to close her right eye fully, has lost the right forehead furrow, and has ipsilateral taste disturbance. There is no limb weakness, no hearing loss, and no skin rash. She presents to A&E 20 hours after onset. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. What is the most appropriate management?
options: MRI brain with gadolinium before starting any treatment
Prednisolone 50 mg once daily for 10 days, started immediately, plus eye protection
Aciclovir 800 mg five times daily alone without steroids
Refer to ENT in 2 weeks as most cases resolve spontaneously
Physiotherapy to the right face to prevent contractures
explanation: Bell's palsy (idiopathic lower motor neurone facial palsy) is diagnosed clinically when upper and lower facial muscles are equally affected, with no other neurological signs and no identifiable cause. Prednisolone started within 72 hours of onset is the evidence-based treatment that significantly increases the rate and completeness of recovery. Eye protection (lubricating drops during the day, tape at night) is essential to prevent exposure keratopathy from incomplete eye closure.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI brain is not routinely required for classic Bell's palsy; it is indicated only when the diagnosis is uncertain (UMN pattern, associated neurological signs, no recovery after 3 months, or history of malignancy).
This is the correct answer — prednisolone (50 mg for 10 days) started within 72 hours is the evidence-based treatment for Bell's palsy, combined with eye protection to prevent corneal damage.
Antivirals alone (without steroids) are not superior to placebo for Bell's palsy; they may be added to steroids in some guidelines but are not the primary treatment.
Waiting 2 weeks to refer is inappropriate as the treatment window for corticosteroids (within 72 hours) will have passed; early treatment maximises recovery.
Physiotherapy is a useful adjunct in prolonged cases but does not address the acute medical management and would be premature before pharmacological treatment.
clinicalPearl: Bell's palsy must be treated with prednisolone within 72 hours of symptom onset for maximum benefit. Always check for eye closure and prescribe eye protection immediately to prevent corneal ulceration.
keyClue: Complete unilateral LMN facial weakness (forehead involved) with taste disturbance, no rash, no hearing loss, presenting within 72 hours
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI brain before starting treatment, which delays the time-sensitive corticosteroid window.
missedConcept: Corticosteroid treatment is time-critical in Bell's palsy; starting after 72 hours significantly reduces benefit. The forehead being involved distinguishes LMN from UMN facial palsy.

subtopic: Interstitial lung disease as a complication
clinicalStem: A 52-year-old woman is admitted with a 4-month history of progressive bilateral proximal upper and lower limb weakness and difficulty rising from a chair. She has no skin rash. CK is 8,400 IU/L, EMG shows myopathic changes with fibrillations, and muscle biopsy reveals endomysial CD8+ T-cell infiltrate. She is diagnosed with polymyositis and started on prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. Two weeks later she develops a dry cough, progressive breathlessness, and new fine bibasal crackles. Her SpO2 falls to 91% on air. What complication has developed and what is the most urgent next investigation?
options: Iatrogenic pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP); bronchoscopy with BAL
Pulmonary embolism; urgent CT pulmonary angiography
Community-acquired pneumonia; sputum culture and chest X-ray only
Interstitial lung disease associated with polymyositis; high-resolution CT chest
Steroid-induced heart failure; urgent echocardiogram
explanation: Interstitial lung disease (ILD) is a well-recognised and serious extra-muscular complication of polymyositis and dermatomyositis, occurring in up to 30-40% of patients, often associated with anti-Jo-1 antibodies. Dry cough, progressive breathlessness, and bibasal crackles developing in a patient with polymyositis should prompt urgent HRCT chest. HRCT is the investigation of choice for characterising the pattern of ILD (e.g., NSIP, UIP) which guides treatment escalation (adding azathioprine, mycophenolate, or cyclophosphamide).
wrongAnswerExplanations: PJP is an important complication of high-dose immunosuppression and must be considered (and prophylaxis discussed), but the subacute 2-week course with bibasal crackles in a known polymyositis patient points more strongly to ILD; HRCT is needed to distinguish.

PE can cause breathlessness and hypoxia but does not cause bibasal crackles; the gradual onset over 2 weeks and inflammatory context makes ILD far more likely; CTPA would be ordered if clinical probability of PE were higher.

Community-acquired pneumonia typically presents more acutely with fever, purulent sputum, and unilateral consolidation; the bilateral fine crackles and subacute course in a polymyositis patient are more consistent with ILD.
This is the correct answer — HRCT chest is the investigation of choice for diagnosing and characterising ILD complicating polymyositis, allowing appropriate treatment escalation.
Steroid-induced heart failure does not typically present with bibasal crackles of this character; echocardiogram is not the first-line investigation for this clinical presentation.
clinicalPearl: Interstitial lung disease is a major extra-muscular complication of polymyositis and dermatomyositis — screen all patients with HRCT and anti-Jo-1 antibodies. Dry cough and breathlessness in a myositis patient demand urgent HRCT.
keyClue: Known polymyositis + new dry cough + bibasal fine crackles + SpO2 91% developing subacutely
commonExamTrap: Attributing the respiratory symptoms entirely to the immunosuppression (PJP or infection) without considering ILD as a primary disease complication.
missedConcept: Polymyositis and dermatomyositis carry significant risk of ILD, which can occur independently of muscle disease activity and requires specific treatment escalation beyond steroids alone.
